target_artifact: packages/opencode/src/tool/tool.ts, packages/opencode/src/tool/*.ts (27 tool files), packages/opencode/src/effect/bridge.ts, packages/opencode/src/acp-next/error.ts, packages/opencode/src/session/tools.ts
explanation: 2/5 — The Effect.orDie pattern destroys all typed error context before it reaches the developer. Three of four scenarios require >30 min to find root cause. The fromUnknownDefect function in acp-next/error.ts literally discards the defect parameter (underscore-prefixed, unused). No tool body includes its own name, operation, or parameters in error messages. The `InvalidArgumentsError` typed error class was designed as a recoverable LLM prompt but is swallowed by the outer Effect.orDie in the wrapping layer before the AI SDK can route it back to the model.

per_scenario.name: JSON.parse() failure inside tool body (e.g. malformed args_used parameter)

current_behavior.what_happens: Three code paths exist: (a) bare JSON.parse without try/catch in propose-plan.ts and similar files → throws SyntaxError synchronously → caught by Effect.gen as a defect via Effect.orDie; (b) JSON.parse inside try/catch that silently swallows error (tool-failure.ts:42-46); (c) Effect.try({ try: () => JSON.parse(...), catch: ... }) in record-stress-wave.ts:48 which creates a typed Error that nullifies the Effect.orDie benefit since the orDie catches even typed errors as defects.
current_behavior.error_trace:
In TUI/CLI: `Promise rejected: SyntaxError: Unexpected token in JSON at position 5
    at JSON.parse (<anonymous>)
    at ...` caught by Effect.runPromise in bridge.ts:71. The `InvalidArgumentsError` message is never produced for schema mismatches because the outer Effect.orDie in tool.ts:146 defeats it.
current_behavior.wrapping_layer_impact: tool.ts:146 applies Effect.orDie to the entire execute wrapper AFTER decode. When InvalidArgumentsError is emitted by the decode step (mapError at line 123-128), the outer orDie at line 146 converts it to a defect. The LLM receives a generic tool failure, NOT the crafted 'Please rewrite the input' message.
current_behavior.EffectBridge_translation: bridge.ts:71 calls Effect.runPromiseExit on the orDie'd effect. On defect, Exit.isSuccess is false, so the run() callback puts the exit.cause into Effect.failCause. The original error is wrapped inside Cause.Die but the structured InvalidArgumentsError tag is lost.

timeline_to_root_cause.event: Developer runs tool call that fails silently in the LLM response. The LLM says 'I got an error running that tool'.
Developer checks the terminal/stdout — sees 'Unhandled Promise rejection' or an Effect runtime error.
Developer opens browser DevTools or checks effect logs. Sees `Cause.Die(SyntaxError: Unexpected token)` with no tool name attribution.
Developer searches for `Effect.orDie` across the codebase — finds 64 occurrences. Realizes the pattern is pervasive.
Developer checks `tool.ts` wrapping layer. Discovers the Effect.orDie sandwich — both tool and wrapper apply orDie.
Developer reads `acp-next/error.ts` — finds `fromUnknownDefect(_defect, ...)` with underscore-prefixed unused parameter.
Developer realizes the original error context is actively discarded. Opens the tool source (e.g. tool-failure.ts), finds bare JSON.parse inside try/catch that silently swallows parse errors.
Developer identifies the exact malformed JSON. Total: ~28 min for a simple parse error.

deficient_elements: Effect.orDie converts typed errors to untyped defects — tool identity is lost
Effect.withSpan on outer wrapper captures tool.name but tool body errors fire BEFORE the span in the inner Effect.gen
fromUnknownDefect in acp-next/error.ts:91 takes _defect as a dead parameter — zero error information propagated to ACP clients
Tool-failure.ts:42 uses silent try/catch on JSON.parse that swallows parse errors entirely
No Effect.try wrapping on JSON.parse in propose-plan.ts and out-of-scope-finding.ts

per_scenario.name: DuckDB write failure (disk full, connection refused, SQL error)
per_scenario.location: record-stress-wave.ts, out-of-scope-finding.ts, tool-failure.ts (DuckDB write not yet merged — per git blame 'Add DuckDB write to _analytics_waves after file write' was rolled back)

current_behavior.what_happens: DuckDB writes are NOT yet in the source (grep confirms zero DuckDB imports in tool bodies). When added per the handoff (option B — DuckDBWritePool), the tool will spawn `duckdb <path> -json -c <sql>` without -readonly. If the subprocess fails: (a) duckdb not installed → ENOENT, (b) filesystem full → write error, (c) SQL error → 'Parser Error: ...', (d) concurrent writer → 'Binder Error: Cannot write to table ...'. These are all caught by Effect.orDie → defect. The error message lacks: which tool, which table (_analytics_waves vs _analytics_findings), which SQL statement, which params, which session.
current_behavior.error_trace:
When DuckDB write IS added, the subprocess spawn will be inside Effect.gen, before the tool's Effect.orDie. Error: `duckdb subprocess exited with code 1
stderr: Error: near line 1: Binder Error: Table "_analytics_waves" does not exist` — passes through Effect.orDie → defect → EffectBridge.runPromise rejects. The developer sees `Promise rejected: Error: duckdb subprocess exited with code 1` with no indication that this came from the `record-stress-wave` tool writing to `_analytics_waves`.
current_behavior.dual_backend_failure_mode: Per the handoff, tools write to DuckDB first AND JSONL. If DuckDB write fails, does the tool continue to JSONL? The handoff doesn't specify. Currently, all operations are inside a single Effect.gen — a DuckDB failure would prevent the JSONL write too, both lost.

timeline_to_root_cause.event: DuckDB-analytics tool call fails. User reports 'tool xyz is broken'.
Developer checks log — sees `Error: duckdb subprocess exited with code 1`. No tool name, no table name, no SQL.
Developer grep-codebase for all duckdb subprocess invocations — finds pipeline.ts, db.duckdb.ts, and potentially 10+ tool files.
Developer tries to reproduce — runs tool manually, sees it fails. Still no indication WHICH SQL caused the issue.
Developer adds console.log or Effect.log to the specific tool's execute function, reruns, identifies the failing SQL.
Developer discovers the table `_analytics_waves` doesn't exist — pipeline.ts init hasn't run yet for this session.
Root cause: pipeline init timing issue — analytical tables created by pipeline.ts, but tool runs before pipeline finishes init. Total: ~45 min.

deficient_elements: DuckDB writes (when added) produce error messages with zero tool/table/operation context
No SQL logging on error — just 'subprocess exited with code 1'
No tool-name prefix in DuckDB error messages — developer must cross-reference by grep
Pipeline init timing dependency — no warning when analytical tables don't exist yet
Dual-backend writes inside a single Effect.gen — DuckDB failure prevents JSONL fallback write
Tool-level Effect.orDie prevents partial-failure recovery (no 'DuckDB failed but JSONL wrote OK' scenario)

per_scenario.name: JSONL write failure (ENOSPC — filesystem full)
per_scenario.location: Every tool that writes JSONL — 27 files all use `fs.writeFileString(path, data, { flag: "a" })`

current_behavior.what_happens: AppFileSystem.writeFileString throws when disk is full. The error propagates through Effect.gen → tool's Effect.orDie → defect. The error message includes the filesystem error but NOT the file path that couldn't be written. Multiple tool failures cascade — each tool call dies, and the developer sees a repeating ENOSPC error with no indication of WHICH file was being written.
current_behavior.error_trace:
`Fiber #293 died: Error: ENOSPC: no space left on device, write'
    at Object.write ...` — No `jsonlPath`, no tool name, no session ID. The `Effect.withSpan("Tool.execute", { attributes: { tool.name } })` on the wrapping layer DOES add span context, but only AFTER the tool's inner Effect.orDie. If the error fires inside the tool body, the span is active but doesn't capture what file path failed.
current_behavior.partial_write_risk: JSONL writes use `{ flag: "a" }`. If the file is truncated mid-write due to ENOSPC, the JSONL file ends with a partial line. On next read (e.g., discover-findings reading artifacts.v1.jsonl), `JSON.parse` on the partial line fails, producing a SECOND error that also gets orDie'd.

timeline_to_root_cause.event: Multiple tool calls start failing. LLM says 'tools aren't working'.
Developer sees repeated `ENOSPC` errors. Knows disk is full but not WHICH files.
Developer checks disk usage with `df -h`. Confirms root volume is full.
Developer needs to find which application files are filling the disk. Runs `du -sh .rig/ docs/json/` to locate the biggest files.
Developer finds giant JSONL files in sessions/, registry/, and findings/ directories.
Developer realizes the JSONL append-pattern never rotates or compresses old entries.
Developer runs report generation, which tries to write summary + archive context. That write ALSO fails with ENOSPC.
Developer frees disk space. Total: ~28 min to find cause (fast because ENOSPC is distinctive), but the real issue (no rotation, no compression, no quota) is systemic.

deficient_elements: ENOSPC error does NOT include the file path in any tool body — developer must infer which tool failed from context
No JSONL rotation or size cap — growing unbounded per session
Partial JSONL line on ENOSPC corrupts the file — subsequent JSON.parse calls fail with orDie
The file path IS available in the closure (jsonlPath variable) but never included in error messages
No disk space preflight check before write operations

per_scenario.name: Schema.Struct parameter mismatch (LLM sends wrong types or missing field)
per_scenario.location: tool.ts:112-130 (decode wrapper), executed for every tool call

current_behavior.what_happens: When the LLM sends arguments that fail the Schema.Struct decode, the wrapping layer produces InvalidArgumentsError (line 122-128) with the tool name and validation detail. This is a TYPED error, designed as a recoverable error with a helpful LLM-facing message. However, the outer `.pipe(Effect.orDie)` at line 146 converts it to a DEFECT before the AI SDK can route it back. The LLM receives a generic tool failure, not the crafted 'Please rewrite the input' prompt.
current_behavior.error_trace: `Unhandled rejection: ToolInvalidArgumentsError { tool: "record_stress_wave", detail: "Expected a string, received undefined at verdict" }` — The error is LITERALLY emitted by Schema.decodeUnknownEffect and wrapped in InvalidArgumentsError with full context. But it's then converted to a defect by Effect.orDie on line 146 before it reaches the AI SDK's error recovery path. The crafted message 'Please rewrite the input so it satisfies the expected schema' is never seen by the LLM.
current_behavior.recoverability_loss: The InvalidArgumentsError class (tool.ts:22-31) has a purpose-built `message` getter designed for LLM recovery: `'The ${this.tool} tool was called with invalid arguments: ${this.detail}.\nPlease rewrite the input so it satisfies the expected schema.'` The Effect.orDie on line 146 prevents this message from ever reaching the LLM. Instead, the LLM sees a generic 'tool call failed' and may hallucinate the correct format.

timeline_to_root_cause.event: LLM calls tool with wrong param types. Tool returns 'failed' to LLM.
Developer reads LLM response: 'I was unable to execute that tool. Let me try again.'
Developer opens DevTools console or server logs. Sees `ToolInvalidArgumentsError` with full detail.
Developer reads tool.ts wrapping layer. Finds InvalidArgumentsError (line 22-31) — a purpose-built recoverable error class.
Developer traces the error path: decode → mapError → InvalidArgumentsError → yield* → caught by outer `.pipe(Effect.orDie)` on line 146.
Developer realizes the orDie on line 146 swallows the InvalidArgumentsError before AI SDK can route it to the LLM as a user-facing message.

Developer identifies fix: InvalidArgumentsError must be caught BEFORE the outer orDie — either restructure the wrapping layer, or pass it through as a typed error that the AI SDK sees as a recoverable tool error.

Developer also discovers the tool-body Effect.orDie adds another layer of complexity — errors in SOME tools are caught by the body orDie, others pass through as typed errors to the wrapper layer. Total: ~25 min.

deficient_elements: Effect.orDie on tool.ts:146 converts InvalidArgumentsError to defect before recovery routing
InvalidArgumentsError was designed as a recoverable LLM-prompt error (message getter) but is never used as one
The wrapping layer's orDie sandwich (line 122-128 decode → mapError, then line 146 orDie) contradicts itself
No distinction between 'recoverable validation error' and 'unexpected system error' in the orDie pattern
Tool-body orDie on each tool compounds the issue — some errors get double-orDie'd, others single-orDie'd

structural_issues.detail: Both the tool body (e.g., tool-failure.ts:67 `.pipe(Effect.orDie)`) and the wrapping layer (tool.ts:146 `.pipe(Effect.orDie, ...)`) apply Effect.orDie. The inner orDie converts tool errors to defects before the wrapper can intercept them. The outer orDie catches even InvalidArgumentsError which was designed as a recoverable typed error. This double conversion means ALL tool errors become unrecoverable defects.

Function signature: `export function fromUnknownDefect(_defect: unknown, safeMessage = "Internal service failure")` — the `_defect` parameter is underscore-prefixed (intentionally unused). When a tool defect propagates through the ACP runtime (acp-next/agent.ts:89), this function is called. The actual error information is DISCARDED and replaced with "Internal service failure". The developer using ACP sees a completely opaque error.

structural_issues.issue: No tool-name attribution in tool body error messages
structural_issues.detail: Every tool execute function has access to `ctx.sessionID`, `ctx.agent`, and the tool's own name (via closure or `id`), but NONE of the 27 tool bodies include these identifiers in error messages. The Effect.withSpan on the wrapper layer (tool.ts:134-146) does annotate with `tool.name`, but this span context is only visible to Effect observer middleware — not in the default error output that a developer debugging a rejected promise sees.

structural_issues.issue: No preflight check on JSONL file paths before writes
structural_issues.detail: All 27 tools call `fs.writeFileString(path, JSON.stringify(record) + "\n", { flag: "a" })`. None check if `path.dirname(path)` has sufficient disk space, if the parent directory exists before ensureDir, or if the file is valid JSONL before appending. A corrupted JSONL file (from a previous ENOSPC partial write) causes JSON.parse failures on subsequent reads.

structural_issues.issue: Effect.tryPromise recommendation in handoff is incorrect
structural_issues.detail: The handoff says 'Use Effect.tryPromise for JSON.parse() and other effectful sync operations' but JSON.parse is synchronous, not a Promise. Effect.tryPromise would work accidentally (Promises auto-resolve synchronous values) but loses stack trace clarity. The correct Effect API is `Effect.try({ try: () => JSON.parse(...), catch: ... })`. The actual code in record-stress-wave.ts:48 does use Effect.try correctly, but other tools like out-of-scope-finding.ts:40 use bare JSON.parse inside unsound try/catch.

repair_recommendations.description: Fix the Effect.orDie sandwich — remove Effect.orDie from tool.ts:146 and keep it only in tool bodies OR only in the wrapper, not both
repair_recommendations.detail: Remove `.pipe(Effect.orDie` from tool.ts:146. Change to: `.pipe(Effect.catchAll((error) => { if (error instanceof InvalidArgumentsError) return Effect.fail(error); return Effect.die(error); }), Effect.withSpan(...))`. This preserves InvalidArgumentsError as a recoverable typed error while still converting unexpected errors to defects.
repair_recommendations.test: Inject a malformed param via unit test — verify LLM receives recoverable error, not defect

repair_recommendations.description: Fix fromUnknownDefect to preserve error context
repair_recommendations.detail: Change `fromUnknownDefect` to extract and serialize error information from the defect: `export function fromUnknownDefect(defect: unknown, safeMessage = "Internal service failure") { const detail = defect instanceof Error ? defect.message : String(defect); return new ServiceFailureError({ safeMessage: `${safeMessage}: ${detail}` }); }`
repair_recommendations.test: Throw a defect from a tool, verify ACP client receives meaningful error message

repair_recommendations.description: Add tool-name context to all Effect.try catch handlers in tool bodies
repair_recommendations.detail: Every Effect.try call inside tool execute functions should include the tool name and operation in the error message. Pattern: `Effect.try({ try: () => JSON.parse(x), catch: (e) => new Error("[tool_name] parsing attacks_attempted: " + e) })`
repair_recommendations.test: Inject parse errors and verify tool-name prefix in error output

repair_recommendations.description: Add file path to ENOSPC/IO error messages in tool bodies
repair_recommendations.detail: When a writeFileString call fails, log or include the file path. Since Effect.orDie strips typed errors, wrap writes in Effect.try with context: `yield* Effect.try({ try: () => fs.writeFileString(path, data), catch: (e) => new Error("[tool_failure] write to " + path + " failed: " + e) })`
repair_recommendations.test: Simulate ENOSPC via test filesystem, verify path appears in error

repair_recommendations.description: Add DuckDB error context when write path is implemented
repair_recommendations.detail: When DuckDBWritePool is added (per handoff option B), each write call must include: tool name, table name, SQL statement, and affected params in the error message. Pattern: `DuckDBWritePool.write("_analytics_waves", record).pipe(Effect.mapError((e) => new Error("[record_stress_wave] DuckDB write to _analytics_waves failed: " + e.message)))`
repair_recommendations.test: Point DuckDB at invalid path, verify error contains tool name + table name + SQL

repair_recommendations.description: Implement JSONL rotation or capped-size per file
repair_recommendations.detail: JSONL files grow unbounded. Add rotation at 10MB per file or 10K entries per file. Include the JSONL file path in log output at write time so post-mortem analysis can correlate failures to specific files.

repair_recommendations.description: Replace Effect.tryPromise recommendation with Effect.try in handoff
repair_recommendations.detail: Update docs/json/opencode/handoffs/tool-conversion-to-builtins.v1.json line 181 to say 'Use Effect.try for synchronous operations, Effect.tryPromise for async operations.' JSON.parse is synchronous — use Effect.try.
